coredata.dc:title: Dynamic synthesis of CdTe NRs: Diameter dependent tuning of PL quenching efficiency for sensitive organic vapor detection
coredata.dc:creator: Kapoor, Sakshi
Ahmad, Hilal
Julien, C.M.
Islam, S.S.
coredata.dc:description: Cadmium telluride (CdTe) is one of the well-explored semiconductors in the history of material science research, because of its innumerable benefits in all dimensional nanostructures. However, no endeavor has been dedicated as of now to understanding the size-dependent tuning of nonradiative emission efficiency and how it influences the sensitive detection of organic vapors (ethanol and acetone) through photoluminescence (PL) quenching mechanism. Herein, the aspect ratio of CdTe nanorods (NRs) is varied by changing its diameter while keeping the axial length constant through electrodeposition into lab made anodic aluminium oxide (AAO) membranes. The growth kinetics and thermodynamics involved while electrodeposition of CdTe NR in each distinct pore diameter of AAO membrane is studied and reasoned. The surface morphology of variable diameter AAO membranes and NRs, respectively fabricated under various electrochemical parameters are observed through a field emission scanning electron microscope. The relation between NRs dimension and structural parameters like d-spacing, crystallite size, band area ratio, and exciton-phonon ratio are extracted from X-ray diffraction and Resonance Raman spectroscopy. The strongly luminescent CdTe NRs, coupled with their chemical durability and organic molecule affinity displayed a sensitive response of ~12 and 6 ppm to ethanol and acetone vapors in the range of 10–100 ppm as a PL sensor. The carrier lifetime measurement through time-correlated single photon counting spectroscopy endorses the role of increased recombination density with a decrease in the diameter of NRs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustrating 3-electrode electrochemical deposition of CdTe into nanoporous AAO membrane at 70 °C with continuous stirring. The curved arrow shows the enlarged view of the working electrode fabricated by adhering gold sputtered AAO membrane on double-sided Cu tape mounted on a glass slide. The graph current density vs electrodeposition time (I-T) recorded using a Solartron 1280 C, depicts three distinct stages through FESEM images plus sketches (shown as inset) and (b) FESEM image of 100 nm NRs with magnified bird’s eye view image in a yellow frame. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

FESEM images of NRs possessing diameter (Dr): (a) 20 nm, (b) 40 nm, (c) 60 nm, (d) 80 nm, (e) 100 nm protruding through the same diameter porous AAO surface (Dp) and (f) side-view portraying growth after 15 min and 35 min (inset), where particles combine together along the length of AAO nanochannels.

Diameter histograms of the electrodeposited CdTe NRs in different diameter AAO membranes: (a) 20 nm, (b) 40 nm, (c) 60 nm, (d) 80 nm and (e) 100 nm. The solid normal distribution fit and blue bars are of protruding NRs represented by the blue marker and magenta bars fitted with dotted distribution curve are of respective AAO membranes depicted by the magenta marker. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Combined XRD spectra as a function of NR diameter and (b) d-spacing plot of (111) plane versus NR diameter.
NRs combined spectra of (a) UV–visible absorption with tauc-plot as inset (following same colour coding in both) and (b) TCSPC with lifetime decay table as inset.

(a) Combined photoluminescence spectra of variable diameter NRs, (b) energy band and molecular structural diagram (red-Cd, blue-Te, white-ethanol and acetone) representing PL quenching phenomenon and (c) percentage PL quenching graph of NRs in the presence of 25 ppm acetone and ethanol, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Investigation of PL intensity variation with LOD values, (b) Stern–Volmer plot of NRs PL with Ksv values; towards ethanol and acetone vapors in 10–100 ppm concentration range and (c) FTIR spectra of NRs exposed to ethanol and acetone vapor with inset displaying crystal structure of CdTe with organic molecule vapors (red-Cd, blue-Te, white-ethanol and acetone). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Dynamic synthesis of CdTe NRs: Diameter dependent tuning of PL quenching efficiency for sensitive organic vapor detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cadmium telluride (CdTe) is one of the well-explored semiconductors in the history of material science research, because of its innumerable benefits in all dimensional nanostructures. However, no endeavor has been dedicated as of now to understanding the size-dependent tuning of nonradiative emission efficiency and how it influences the sensitive detection of organic vapors (ethanol and acetone) through photoluminescence (PL) quenching mechanism. Herein, the aspect ratio of CdTe nanorods (NRs) is varied by changing its diameter while keeping the axial length constant through electrodeposition into lab made anodic aluminium oxide (AAO) membranes. The growth kinetics and thermodynamics involved while electrodeposition of CdTe NR in each distinct pore diameter of AAO membrane is studied and reasoned. The surface morphology of variable diameter AAO membranes and NRs, respectively fabricated under various electrochemical parameters are observed through a field emission scanning electron microscope. The relation between NRs dimension and structural parameters like d-spacing, crystallite size, band area ratio, and exciton-phonon ratio are extracted from X-ray diffraction and Resonance Raman spectroscopy. The strongly luminescent CdTe NRs, coupled with their chemical durability and organic molecule affinity displayed a sensitive response of ~12 and 6 ppm to ethanol and acetone vapors in the range of 10–100 ppm as a PL sensor. The carrier lifetime measurement through time-correlated single photon counting spectroscopy endorses the role of increased recombination density with a decrease in the diameter of NRs.

ce:section.ce:para: Cadmium Telluride (CdTe) is a luminescent II-VI semiconductor exhibiting exemplary optical, physical, and electronic properties. The features of the material glorify on scaling down to a few nanometers from bulk or thin films due to quantum confinement, strain, surface defects, etc. The low-dimensional CdTe nanostructures have always attracted attention in the field of photovoltaic devices, biological labels, and other electronic devices because it offers a broad spectrum of bandgap energy in the visible range [1]. For instance, the devices based on a single CdTe nanowire (NW) by making electrical contacts via nanolithography are alluring for fabricating one-dimensional (1D) quantum structure devices [2]. The high-density growth of CdTe nanostructures of controllable diameter is vital for the better execution of nanoscale devices. Several synthesis techniques had been developed in the past, including solution-based chemical synthesis, self-assembly from nanoparticles, chemical vapor deposition, and with and without template-assisted method [3].

Template directed electrodeposition is valued as a systematic approach for making one-dimensional nanostructures because of its ease in managing deposition conditions. Additionally, low cost and facility to alter the dimensions of anodic aluminium oxide (AAO) template is another advantage [4,5]. This robust AAO template available in different diameters is widely used in a variety of applications like humidity sensing [6]. Moreover, AAO template directed 1D nanostructure array has potential device applications in a supercapacitor, battery, solar water splitting due to its following benefits like large scale implementation, high regularity, tunable nanoscale structural quality, predefined spatial orientation, and efficient 1D electron transport [7]. The studies of CdTe nanorods (NRs) were carried out at different deposition voltages through track-etched polycarbonate, AAO membrane [8,9], and on different conducting substrates like ITO [10], while analysis of variable diameter CdTe NRs is scarce in literature so far. The research on different diameter 1D CdTe nanostructures liberated through polycarbonate membranes [11], was executed on large diameters (100 nm-2 µm).

Organic solvents like ethanol and acetone possess wide industrial and household applications but are volatile in nature. These chemicals easily vaporize at room temperature (RT) and exposure to them may have dangerous health effects. Therefore, it is of utmost importance to develop an efficient, sensitive, and swift technique plus materials for the measurement of these organic vapors in the health sector. There are diversified strategies available for monitoring hazardous molecules, which include electrical, optical, and quartz crystal microbalance [12]. The technique used in the optical method includes photoluminescence, UV–visible absorption, surface plasmon resonance, and time-correlated single photon counting spectroscopy (TCSPC). Photoluminescence (PL) quenching offers usefulness over other methods such as accuracy, sensitivity, reproducibility, safety and is an easy-to-use probe technique [13]. Luminescent semiconductor nanostructures exhibit size dependent spectral properties like narrow and tunable emission peaks and broad absorption bands that have been used in spectroscopic research and applications [14]. Moreover, the resistance to photobleaching, chemical stability, and water solubility make them more interesting fluorescent probes than traditional organic dyes for chemo/biosensing/drug delivery applications [15]. The red fluorescence of CdTe QDs was efficiently quenched by cardiolipin [16], molecular oxygen [17], Au nanocrystals [15], cocaine structure [18], and many more in the past. The drawback associated with QDs as fluorophore is low yield, random orientation, and agglomeration; therefore, 1D nanostructured CdTe material may be a good choice to be explored in this field. However, there are very few reports on using quantum-sized CdTe NRs as a fluorophore, micron size NRs are used for the detection of heavy metal ions [19].

Recently, many nanostructures were explored for the efficient sensing of ethanol and acetone through the gas sensing technique at a particular operating temperature range. Cauliflower shaped Bi2O3-ZnO heterojunction displayed superior sensing performance towards 1 ppm ethanol at 175 °C [20], ZnO@ZnS heterostructure had a high response towards 10 ppm ethanol at 325 °C [21], Pt-doped ZnO nanoplates showed great selectivity towards 2 ppm ethanol at 400 °C [22]; in comparison to pure ZnO nanostructures. Similarly, doped (Al, Cu, Co) ZnO nanoparticles exhibited better sensitivity than undoped ZnO toward 1 ppm acetone in the temperature range 300–550 °C for breath analyzer application [23]. Fe2O3 @Co3O4 double-shelled nanotubes had high conductivity towards 100 ppm ethanol at 160 °C [24], whereas pristine Co3O4 nanofibers showed rapid response and recovery time towards 100 ppm acetone at 190 °C [25]. The enhanced sensing response towards 5 ppm ethanol at 300 °C is observed by TiO2/Ag2V4O11 nanoheterostructure [26], and Ag modified TiO2 nanobelts showed more stability towards 10 ppm acetone at temperature range 240–340 °C [27]; vis-à-vis pure TiO2. The amplified response towards 20 ppm ethanol is exhibited by ZnO-SnO2-Zn2SnO4 nanofibers at 130 °C [28], and Pt-Zn2SnO4 hollow octahedra detected acetone in the breath of diabetic patients in ppb levels at 350 °C [29]; than pure Zn2SnO4.

In this article, the synthesis of AAO template based CdTe NRs of variable diameters is reported with their electrochemical growth and lattice dynamics thoroughly studied from structural plus optical spectroscopy. The interaction involved behind fundamental and overtone Raman modes, excited through different incoming resonance energies are discussed via mathematical derivations for the NRs possessing crystallite size smaller than the Bohr exciton diameter of CdTe. We have found that the adsorption of organic molecules on the surface of CdTe NRs systematically alters its electronic band gap, depending upon the delocalization of electrons. The interrelation between NR diameter, surface defect density, and tuning of nonradiative emission efficiency plays a decisive role in organic vapor sensing; and to the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on CdTe so far. The vapor sensing is carried out through the PL quenching technique at room temperature. Furthermore, the results showed that CdTe NRs could be employed as an ideal luminescent organic vapor probe. The achieved experimental results are also correlated with density functional theory (DFT) analysis on CdTe nanostructures presented by other authors in the recent past [30–32].

All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Aluminum (Al) sheet, Platinum (Pt) mesh and Tellurium dioxide (TeO2) from Alfa Aesar; Cadmium sulfate hydrate ((CdSO4)3.8H2O), Cupric chloride (CuCl2.2H2O), Acetone (C3H6O), Toluene (C7H8) and Xylene (C8H10) from Thermo Fisher Scientific; Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), Oxalic acid (C2H2O4), Hydrochloric acid (HCl), Ethanol (C2H5OH), Phosphoric acid (H3PO4), Benzene (C6H6), Distilled (DI) water and Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) pellets from Merck India, were procured. Dry commercial grade nitrogen (N2) gas cylinder and N-type Silicon (Si) wafer (100) were supplied from Sigma. Reference Electrode K0265 Ag/AgCl [3 mol L¯1 KCl] was bought from Princeton Applied Research. Electroplating and Copper (Cu) tape were purchased from Johnson Tape Pvt. Ltd, India.

Through-hole AAO membranes of variable pore diameter were fabricated using the approach explained in our previous report [33], with slight modification. A two-step anodization process in strong and mild acids (H2SO4, C2H2O4, H3PO4) possessing a pH of 1–4, results in the better ordering of pores [34]. The anodization parameters to prepare different size nanochannels are listed in Table S1; during the process, if stirring of the electrolyte is stopped the reaction heat accelerates either chemical dissolution or oxide formation [35], causing a rise in current density (Fig. S1). The bare free-standing AAO membrane was used intact in the ion-diffusion technique whereas in electrodeposition it is essential to make it conducting. Therefore, gold (Au) film (100–300 nm) was DC sputtered onto one side of the membrane to provide it with a conductive contact. Further, the coated membrane was mounted on the glass slide by adhering to the double-sided copper tape which acts as a working electrode. The digital images displaying transition from bare Al sheet to AAO membrane electrode are illustrated in Fig. S2.

The potentiostatic cathodic deposition of CdTe was carried out in the same way as that described previously [36]. In brief, CdTe was deposited into the pores of an AAO membrane from an aqueous electrolyte containing 0.05 mol L¯¹ (CdSO4)3.8H2O and 0.0005 mol L¯¹ TeO2 in 1 mol L¯¹ H2SO4, and adjusting the pH = 2 by using 2 mol L¯¹ NaOH. An acidic bath is preferable over an alkaline bath, as it yields poor plus powdery deposit and Te is stable only at pH< 7. Further, within acidic bath pH = 2 is preferable over pH = 1 as HTeO2⁺ reduction is unfavorable in the former [8]. The deposition was realized using a 3-electrode system in a glass cell with AAO membrane as working, Pt. mesh as counter, and Ag/AgCl as reference electrode; applying − 0.598 V for 50 min at 70 °C to achieve near stoichiometric CdTe NRs. However, a potential range below − 0.40 V versus Ag/AgCl leads to overpotential electrodeposition of Te-rich NRs and a potential range beyond − 0.60 V versus Ag/AgCl leads to overpotential electrodeposition of Cd-rich NRs [8]. The scheme illustrating electrodeposition of CdTe into the nanochannels of AAO membrane is shown in Fig. 1a. The electrolyte was confined to the bare side of the membrane so that CdTe growth was started onto the Au seed layer from within the pores. Finally, the membrane was dissolved after deposition in 2 mol L¯¹ NaOH for 30 min at 50 °C to suspend NRs from the membrane; for further characterization.

The organic vapor photoluminescence quenching experiments were accomplished in the same way as reported earlier by our group; performed on porous Si samples [13,37]. Briefly, CdTe film (different diameter NRs) deposited on Si wafer was initially placed in a sensor chamber for exposure to organic solvent vapors, while the molecules were transported by N2 gas through vapor chamber. After 10 min incubation of organic vapor exposure on NRs film, the PL quenching titrations were carried out. The laser beam was pointed onto the sensor through a confocal microscope and spectra were recorded accordingly. The quenching experiments were performed at room temperature (27 °C) and humidity ∼ 54% RH.

The morphology was observed using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM: Nova NanoSEM 450, FEI and SIGMA, ZEISS). The absorption energy band was acquired using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Lambda 35, Perkin-Elmer) having a path length of 1 cm at a scan rate of 120 nm min−1. Raman spectra and photoluminescence quenching results were obtained using a micro-Raman spectrometer (Lab RAM HR 800, Horiba) through Olympus 100x objective lens having a spot size of 1.19 µm; excitation wavelength of 514 nm and 4 mW optical power of argon ion laser. X-ray diffractograms were recorded using an X-ray diffractometer (Smart Lab XRD, Rigaku) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) at a scan rate of 0.02° s¯¹ . Time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) measurements were done using 529 nm diode laser excitation (Horiba, Delta Diode). The functionalized groups attached were studied through Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Vertex70 V, Bruker Optik). Anodization was performed using Aplab DC power supply H0310, Au metal was coated using DC sputter unit (Denton Vacuum) for 120 s at 50 mA, and electrochemical deposition was carried out using Solartron 1280 C. Lorentzian peak fitting is used throughout for all spectroscopic results and calculations.

Prior to the use of quasi-one-dimensional CdTe semiconductor nanostructures for industrial applications, its in-depth analysis is essential for device fabrication. Although, quantum confinement effects are more profound in nanostructures below 20 nm, while other reasons contributing to the blue shift on decreasing the diameter of NRs (100–20 nm) are discovered in subsequent sections below.

Fig. 1a shows the plot of current density versus time (electrodeposition performed by using the set-up shown in the scheme), which reveals three distinct zones of the pore filling process. The initial transient drop in current marks the nucleation stage for the growth of CdTe (Zone I), which is the steady-state phase for the formation of NRs. Zone II exhibits a rapid rise in current as a consequence of filled pores with deposited material. The hemispherical caps are formed at the tip of each NR as further deposition results in the overgrowth of reactant species onto the tip. This is because the electric field and current density lines amid the tip of protruding NRs from the pores and the counter electrode are more than that between both electrodes due to a shorter gap. The current becomes constant in Zone III as the caps merge into a planar and continuous film [38]. The relatively lower range of current density is due to the presence of ~20 µm thick diffusion layer between the pores and the gold electrode surface which leads to a higher resistive path for the flow of ions, irrespective of the fact that the bath was magnetically stirred [8]. The curve follows a similar pattern of electrodeposition in all pore diameters of the AAO membrane [39–42], with current densities ranging from 18 to 10 mA cm−2 for 100–20 nm diameter, respectively.

The growth of NWs or NRs (Fig. 1b) rather than nanotubes (NTs) is due to the presence of thick Au layer coating (ca. 200 nm) inside the pore walls making them highly conductive [43,44]. The deposition current density is relatively high (18–10 mA) in the case of sputtered pore walls as compared to bottom pores bonded on conducting substrates [36,45]. On the onset of the applied electric field, Cd²⁺ and HTeO2⁺ diffuse through the electrolyte and reduce along the charged pore walls of the AAO membrane electrode, forming CdTe NTs [36]. After the initial deposition, the membrane electrode is isolated from the electrolyte by the deposit and an electric current passes through the deposit to permit further deposition activity. The reduction or oxidation process happens between the electrode and electrolyte molecular orbitals till equilibrium is achieved [38]. Further, an increase in electrodeposition duration and higher current density convert NTs into NRs or NWs by filling inside the pores. The electrodeposition is affected by various factors such as overpotential, concentration, temperature, pH, and additives. High concentration leads to larger grains and less nucleation whereas a large number of smaller grains and nuclei are formed when the concentration is low [46]. The suppressed growth kinetics and thermodynamics along the channel length than lateral growth rate at lower ion concentration results in NRs having smaller diameter because of the gap between NRs and pore walls [47]. This is also due to insufficient supply of ions which causes its depletion in the diffusion layer under a diffusion-limited atmosphere along the axial direction of long and narrow pores [48].

Before anodization, surface polishing was carried out to enhance self-ordering of the porous structure; optimized and over-etched micrographs are presented in Figs. S3 and S4. The surface topography of different pore diameter AAO membranes (lab made) is illustrated in Fig. S5. Further, large dumps formed due to high current density while stirring off in anodization are shown in Fig. S6. The NRs liberated from the AAO membrane are vertically aligned as shown in Fig. 2(a-e), corresponding closely to the pore diameter and depth with high packing density throughout the area (1 cm2) of the membrane [33]. The liberated NRs persist in the self-standing array structure despite the bundling, because of the capillary effect during the wet-to-dry etching process of the AAO membrane [49]. The growth mechanism discussed in Section 3.1. is confirmed by the FESEM images shown in Fig. 2f, where growth initially starts along the pore walls and nanoparticles combine to form NRs. Therefore, a lower concentration of electrolyte results in rough and dendritic morphology of nanobelts; NRs fill the pores uniformly under precursor ratio difference of 100 that is, optimized concentration whereas a high amount leads to film growth on AAO membrane [50,51] as shown in Fig. S7. In addition, the influence of electrolyte bath pH value on the growth conditions is shown in Fig. S8.

The NRs diameter (Dr) is in good agreement with the AAO membrane pore diameter (Dp) used for the electrodeposition [52] and analogous with theoretically desired diameter, as confirmed by statistical distribution shown in Fig. 3. A histogram was drawn by taking into account various regions of each sample from the FESEM micrographs of NRs (Fig. 2) and equivalent AAO membranes (Fig. S5); to determine the average size distributions, examined by Image J software [52]. The pattern of deposition observed with increasing diameter is reviewed in the following cases:

Case 1: As shown in Fig. 3(a-b), NRs exhibit a narrow size distribution with a much smaller average diameter (14.9 ± 2.0 and 36.8 ± 6.3 nm) compared to the membrane pores (25.8 ± 5.3 and 49.7 ± 7.9 nm) for 20 and 40 nm diameter, respectively. When the growth of CdTe NRs is restricted in AAO diameter slightly more than Bohr exciton diameter to witness quantum confinement effect, the pore walls inhibit consistent axial as well as lateral direction propagation. In cylindrical NRs, length is more than the diameter, suggesting confinement effect to occur in the lateral (diameter) direction. Hence, this is the reason for obtaining reduced dimension NRs; owing to incomplete pore filling but uniform narrow size distribution [47].

Case 2: As shown in Fig. 3c, NRs present an average diameter of approximately 62.0 ± 7.8 nm resembling the diameter (65.8 ± 9.0 nm) of the AAO membrane. The distribution pattern of NRs overlaps the pore distribution of the membrane, dictating that complete pore filling is achieved at 60 nm diameter [52].

Case 3: As shown in Fig. 3(d-e), the average diameter of NRs (90.6 ± 10.1 and 126.4 ± 13.2 nm) is particularly larger than the average pore diameter (85.7 ± 12.7 and 119.3 ± 15.4 nm) for 80 and 100 nm diameter, respectively. A broad distribution fit and more than± 10 standard deviation relate to complete pore filling but uneven size distribution. The reason for this can be devoted to the pronounced diffusion-limited process which causes head on collision of nuclei with other growing nuclei before meeting the pore walls [47]. Additionally, the structural bending of the NRs under their weight also increases the diameter than the membrane pore size.

Thus, we conclude coherent and dense pore filling of the AAO membrane at 60 nm, as investigated by comparing the diameter distribution of NRs to the membrane pores. Further, many nucleation sites are accessible like that at a planar interface in large diameter (80–100 nm) vis-à-vis a few or even one nucleation site is accountable for the growth of any given NR in small diameter (20–40 nm) AAO membranes [47].

The diffraction planes (111), (220), (311), and (331) of CdTe cubic phase structure (JCPDS Card: 015-0770) were observed in all diameter NRs dispersed on a glass slide with (111) as the preferential growth direction ( Fig. 4a) [8,36]. The (201) reflection in the pattern might be due to the presence of hexagonal phase Te adatoms (JCPDS Card: 036-1452), where the probability of finding them is more in the core rather than along the axial direction of NRs [36]. Naderi et al. carried out DFT ab-initio computational study of Cd and Te adatoms diffusion on (111) surface of zinc blend (cubic phase) CdTe crystal, as adatoms can tune the electronic properties of the devices [31]. The results suggested that CdTe (111) growth appears to be smooth, stoichiometric, and defect-free under extra Cd partial pressure, as the interaction of Te (5.2 eV) is stronger than Cd (0.39 eV) with A- and B-type CdTe. Practically, annealing in the presence of CdCl2 removes all excess elemental Te from the surface of nanostructures and thin films. This is due to the chemical reaction with Cd from CdCl2 and sublimation during heat treatment; improving stoichiometry and crystallinity [53].

The crystallite size (D) and inter-atomic spacing (d) is determined by Debye-Scherrer and Bragg’s formula due to the absence of significant peak broadening and change in 2Ɵ position, therefore disclosing less contribution of strain [54]. The high value of d-spacing decreases with increasing NR diameter (Fig. 4b), as the growth of CdTe is restricted because of spatial confinement applied by the AAO membrane [47]. Basically, beyond 15–20 nm pore diameter, D and d-spacing appear to be approximately constant or drop slightly with increasing pore diameter (20–100 nm); reflecting an increase in lattice distortion. Further, growth through electrodeposition produces nanostructures possessing little strain because of lattice mismatch between the substrate and deposition ions; creating a new phase at low temperatures. Note that epitaxy allows defect-free growth, as it is mainly mediated by van der Waals interaction rather than covalent chemical bonds [47]. However, as-grown NRs through electrodeposition appear to be nanocrystalline at 70 °C and amorphous at RT as shown in Fig. S9.

During Raman spectroscopy, laser excitation (λ = 514 nm) distorts the ionic lattice resulting in phonon excitation that is ascribed as crystalline modes, with longitudinal optical phonon (LO) mode making the progression up to second order (2LO) in all diameter NRs. The peaks of small diameter NRs are broad, weak, and feeble in comparison to large diameter NRs as illustrated in near-resonant Raman spectra ( Fig. 5a). The reason for more pronounced Te modes (A1 and E) than transverse optical (TO) and LO phonon modes can be attributed to Te adatoms on the surface of the NRs or photo-chemical reactions involved in vacancy relocations [55]. The electron-phonon interaction is mainly originated from two mechanisms: the deformation and Frӧhlich potential. The LO mode has a substantially greater amplification factor than the TO mode, indicating a dominating Frӧhlich electron-phonon coupling (Fig. 5a). This mechanism involves the long-range interaction generated by displacement of the partially ionic nuclei [56]. The coupling strength of the electron-phonon interaction can be determined by comparing the relative intensities of the overtones to the fundamental Raman band. The ratio of the first overtone intensity to the fundamental mode (I(2LO)/I(1LO)) and band area ratio (A(2LO)/A(LO)) exhibits the same increases from 0.21 to 1.2 and 0.28–1.16 with an increasing diameter as shown in Fig. 5b and inset. This is because exciton-vibration coupling lowers in densely packed smaller diameter NRs [33]. The LO and 2LO phonon frequencies were found to be red-shifted by 10 and 12 cm¯¹ for the 20 nm samples than 100 nm NRs in Fig. 5c, due to the intrinsic nature of semiconducting NRs at smaller diameters possessing higher tensile strain [47,57].

In 1992, our group [32] performed Raman scattering experiments on pure CdTe crystal oriented along (110) face, under the temperature range of 17–200 K. The results demonstrated an enhancement of the LO mode with decreasing temperature (17 K) and under resonant energy (2.54 eV, 488 nm) excitation. Similar attempts were made on 1D CdTe NTs to see the upshot of decreasing temperature on LO mode frequency in our earlier reports [3]. In the present work, the effect of different resonant energies on the fundamental mode of CdTe NRs is observed. Martin’s mechanism [58] described that the intraband Frӧhlich interaction scatters the electron and hole from one excitonic state to another within the same valence and conduction bands by generating or annihilating a LO phonon. The intraband Frӧhlich interaction is defined as:

In some cases, multi-phonon Raman scattering [56] in CdTe arises when the incoming photon resonates with the fundamental bandgap (785 nm~1.583 eV), but here we observed a spin-orbital split off in the valence band energy levels. This was confirmed by the enhancement of LO mode at 2.54 eV, 488 nm (Einc~Eo+∆o) resonant energy than 2.41 eV, 514 nm (Einc<Eo+∆o) at RT (Fig. 5e), where Einc= incoming resonance energy and ∆o = spin orbit valence band split off energy. Whence, the same magnitude of enhancement at LO mode was observed in low dimensional 20 nm NR at RT analogous to the amount monitored in CdTe bulk crystal at 17 K [32]. The spin orbital split of valence band energy levels can be attributed to the quantum confinement effect observed in NRs at a single particle level. The calculated crystallite size (D~10 nm) ≤ Bohr exciton diameter (B~14 nm) of CdTe. It is believed that if D/B< <2, then the crystal reveals a single particle confinement behavior in which electrons and holes are confined separately [59]. We certainly observed higher lattice compression and strain on reducing the diameter of NRs from the above structural features; which would help understand the organic vapor detection sensitivity as a function of NR diameter [54].

Improved optical properties are obtained when the growth of CdTe is oriented along the (111) facet [31], as interpreted from as-synthesized CdTe NRs. The exciton energy of nanostructures is necessary to assess many of their optical and electronic properties. The bandgap of CdTe semiconductor can be regulated by reducing its particle size especially below the excitonic Bohr radius of 7.5 nm [36]. The low-energy optical absorbance edge varies significantly (∼1.58–1.62 eV) with changing the diameter of NRs as confirmed from Tauc-plot shown in Fig. 6a inset. The slight blue shift observed in absorbance of lower diameter CdTe NRs can be attributed to inbuilt strain in these NRs grown on porous alumina (Fig. 6a). So, these results demonstrate that surface traits even modify bulk physical properties (e.g., density of states, optical absorption- and emission- band gap) of nanostructures [54].

It is notable to say that the formation of defects on NRs surface is diameter dependent, while average defects in the bulk of NRs remain more or less the same due to growth under similar conditions. Therefore, it is imperative to know the free carrier lifetime which is the combination of radiative and non-radiative decay rates, and this information will help to explain the degree of luminescence quenching [60]. The behaviour of exciton lifetime decay with increasing diameter of NRs is shown in Fig. 6b. The average lifetime (τavg) rises on increasing in diameter due to more delocalization of electron wave function resulting in reduced electron-hole wave function overlap [61]. In the case of smaller diameter NRs, the surface-to-volume ratio increases where surface defects, scattering, traps, adatoms, neighboring atoms offer substitute relaxation pathways for the de-excitation of photoexcited carriers at a rapid rate [60]; thus leading to the shortening of the excited state lifetime values (Fig. 6b inset). As a result, faster quenching results and high photocurrent response are achieved in them due to dipole-dipole interaction of the adjacent particles; enabling resonant energy transfer between nanoparticles in thin NRs [62,63]. The interaction of size and surface effects (defects and scattering) on exciton recombination kinetics in CdTe NRs is better understood through this study.

Experimentally, it is observed in the literature that surface passivation removes unwanted surface states within the bandgap formed due to dangling bonds and adatoms, thereby raising the band gap values [64]. On the contrary, Sarkar et al. theoretically examined a minute enhancement of only 0.06 eV between hydrogen atoms passivated and unpassivated CdTe NWs [30]. The protonation process through passivating agent prevents electron transfer of the excited state; strong influence is seen on the electronic band structure of quantum dots vis-à-vis other nanostructures, resulting in local state PL emission [65]. Since, our work does not involve any passivating agent (additives) during the growth of 1D CdTe NRs, therefore, PL and quenching phenomena are observed due to (111) cubic phase plane of CdTe which contains Cd and Te atoms with one dangling bond each [31]. The PL peak position systematically blue shifts over a range of 0.2 eV photon energies with decreasing diameter of NRs; due to quantum confinement effect induced by the membrane strain ( Fig. 7a). The Raman and PL measurements were done on film of NRs made on crystalline Si wafer in lieu of doing on AAO membrane; to avoid the background luminescence caused by the membrane due to oxygen functionalities attached on them (Fig. S10) [33].

The mechanisms involved behind quenching have been outlined based on fluorescence energy transfer [FRET], electron transfer, vapor pressure, dielectric constant, and dipole moment of the molecules being adsorbed [13]. The CdTe NRs are less electronegative vis-à-vis ethanol and acetone, therefore, have more affinity towards electrons than the former; Fig. 8b depicts the PL quenching system through energy band and molecular diagram. The related surface dangling bonds and adatoms of CdTe NRs make bonding with the molecules of ethanol and acetone, thereby altering the electronic bandgap and optical properties of CdTe NRs. Further, organic molecules (acceptors) adsorbed on the surface of CdTe NRs (donor) form surface traps above the valence band (VB) and attract electrons from the conduction band (CB) of the donor, thereby resulting in PL quenching and emission enhancement [15]. The absence of a new peak in the quenched emission spectra confirms electron transfer to be the sensing mechanism rather than the energy transfer process [66].

In the course of PL quenching, the following are the findings:
(a)
PL of larger diameter NRs is less affected than smaller diameter under similar conditions; implying that quenching of NRs is strongly size-dependent as presented in Fig. 7c. The existence of Te adatoms and lattice compression seen in structural measurements (Section 3.3.) plus more surface defects displayed in TCSPC results (Fig. 6b) at smaller diameter NRs, affirms the above finding.
The percentage quenching (Q%) was evaluated from [66]:

where I and I0 are the PL emission intensities of the NRs in the presence and absence of ethanol and acetone vapors, respectively. The decrease in Q% of NRs luminescence from 49% to 30% and 43% to 28% for 25 ppm of ethanol and acetone vapors, respectively is seen as a function of decreasing diameter.
(b)
PL of the film is quenched more in the presence of ethanol vapors than acetone but sensitivity was found high towards acetone than ethanol due to a higher quenching value [37] of the former as shown in Fig. 8(a-b). This is due to the following factors:
(i)
electronegativity of oxygen in alcohol (O-H, hydroxyl group) is higher than acetone (CO, carboxyl group) therefore, it will withdraw more electrons from CB of CdTe than acetone. As a result, less quenching is observed in acetone vis-à-vis ethanol (Fig. 8c).
(ii)
In addition, the vapor pressure (molar mass of vapor molecules) of ethanol is higher than acetone therefore, more quenching is observed in the former [37].
(a)
The theoretical readings for the organic vapor detection ability of CdTe NRs in terms of detection limit (LOD), Stern–Volmer constant (KSV) is presented in Fig. 8(a-b). The LOD of CdTe film-based probe towards organic vapor is quantified using the relation of change in PL intensity (Io-I) against its concentration, ranging from 10 to 100 ppm (signal to noise ratio: 3) (Fig. 8a). The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated using [66]:

where k = 3; SD represents the standard deviation in the change in emission intensity and b is slope of the regression line. The emission drop in presence of vapor can be fitted with the linear regression equation as:

(5) ∆I = I0-I= 0.20027 C, R2 = 0.98312, (Acetone)
(Where C stands for organic vapor concentration in ppm) as evidence in Fig. 8a. The CdTe NRs possessing SD of± 1.0220 and± 0.4106, delivered a very low LOD of about 12 and 6 ppm for ethanol and acetone, respectively.

PL intensity of NRs eventually quenched with the increase in ethanol and acetone vapor concentration (10–100 ppm) as illustrated in Fig. S11, with a clear red shift indicating nonradiative emission.
(c)
In addition, the selectivity of CdTe NRs towards ethanol and acetone vapors is shown in Fig. S12 by taking into account the relative quenching efficiency of NRs towards other organic vapors; where the surface of the NRs is not modified.

In this work, we explored the synthesis and application of variable diameter CdTe NRs with extensive spectroscopic studies to analyze the size dependent lattice dynamics. The diameter distribution pattern of AAO membrane and NRs revealed good agreement between the theoretical and experimental values. The structural investigations revealed that d-spacing decreases or remains nearly the same with increasing NR diameter. In addition, the relation between frequency position, integrated intensity, and area ratio as a function of NR diameter was also investigated. The spatial confinement by AAO membrane during the growth imposed a blue shift in absorption and PL emission properties for the NRs ranging from 100 to 20 nm. The examination of CdTe NRs as a stable inorganic fluorophore, directed the authors to explore them as commercial device grade sensors in near future. The observations on diameter dependent tuning of nonradiative emission efficiency during PL quenching led to sensible detection of organic vapors; where detection limit was also found dependent on the type of molecules. This is a unique study made for the first time on CdTe NRs, applied in the field of optical sensors.
